slides.narration: Quick tour of the GitExplain codebase. By the end you'll know what every file does, how a slide becomes narrated audio, and where to add your own slide types.

data.title: Tour of the GitExplain codebase
data.subtitle: A local server that turns JSON lessons into narrated slide videos

slides.narration: Three things this repo does. One server, one player, one JSON contract between them.

data.heading: What GitExplain does, in three moves
items: Accepts a lesson JSON over HTTP and stores it in memory.
Streams narration for each slide from ElevenLabs Flash v2.5.
Plays the slides in a browser with synchronized audio and prefetch.

slides.narration: Here's the whole repo. Only seven files matter — server, the four-file player in public, the sample lesson, and the README.
Three layers. The browser renders slides and asks for audio. The Node server stores lessons and proxies ElevenLabs. ElevenLabs runs the speech model.
What happens when a slide plays. The audio element asks the server for an mp3. The server streams from ElevenLabs and tees the bytes — back to the browser and into a cache.
The hot path. This is the audio route that proxies ElevenLabs and caches the result. Notice the highlighted lines — the response is written to the client and collected in memory at the same time.

data.heading: The hot path: /audio/:id/:idx.mp3
data.caption: server.js — proxying and caching in one pass

slides.narration: And the prefetch trick that makes slide transitions feel instant. While slide N plays, the player fires background fetches for N plus one and N plus two, so the server caches them before they're needed.
Want to add a new slide type? Five small edits. Everything stays colocated in player.js and player.css.

data.heading: How to add a 21st slide type

steps.title: Pick a name
steps.detail: Lowercase camelCase, e.g. timelineRange. This is the value of slide.type.

steps.title: Add a renderer
steps.detail: One entry in the RENDERERS map in public/player.js. Takes (data, slide), returns HTML.

steps.title: Style it
steps.detail: Add a .s-yourtype block at the bottom of public/player.css.

steps.title: Document the shape
steps.detail: Add a row to the table in README.md.

steps.title: Write an example
steps.detail: Append a slide to sample-lesson.json so you can eyeball it.

slides.narration: That's the whole codebase. One server, one player, twenty slide types, and a JSON contract. Next stop, wrapping this in an MCP tool any agent can call.

data.heading: Takeaways
points: server.js is a thin Express proxy with in-memory lesson + audio caches.
public/player.js holds all 20 renderers — one function each.
Flash v2.5 + prefetch + tee-to-cache = near-instant slide transitions.
Next: an MCP tool create_lesson(title, slides) that posts to /api/lessons.
